sentences.text: Yes.
Never forget it.
Novosibirskoi ?] [INAUDIBLE].
That's the name of the place where we were in Siberia.
14 months.
Approximately.
A couple days more, a couple days less.
I didn't keep a diary to-- to be exact, the day and the night, but it will stay with me for the rest of my life, whatever I went through.
Well, not much.
Not much.
The people that I worked with, 10 people-- we were a group of 10 people, young people, they wanted to work.
We decided to work, not to-- we wanted to work.
We wanted to survive.
We wanted to.
So we were working very hard and-- just to survive.
And social life, practically none.
You were-- I don't remember if they ever did some kind of a-- mostly work, and work, and work, and just go home and go to the kitchen.
And get, after work, there was the tools that we use to cut the-- there was a saw and a hammer and a chisel and a-- that's all.
I mean, and after we came back from work, we had to return this to the-- to the-- it's called the [? instrumentalka ?], the place where you return your tools-- excuse me-- where they sharpen it.
And there were a couple guys working in that in that place.
And in the morning, we went there.
I remember one incident.
We had a elderly man, a Jewish man from [?
Bielsk. ?] His name was [INAUDIBLE], and he was religious, more than all of us.
He got up in the morning before we went to work, and he put on the tefillin, and he went to work.
He went sooner.
And he encountered a bear, and then he just dropped everything, and everything fell out of his hands, and he-- he fell back.
You know?
The bear didn't do him-- nothing.
He just went by-- nothing, absolute nothing.
And that's very rare because normally a bear hibernates in the winter.
But somehow he got out there because there were a lot of Russian people, professional hunters, that we worked with for a while.
They came to hunt bears.
And one time a little bear came into the-- where the horses were because that's how they took the lumber that we cut, with the sled and horses, and they pulled it to a river.
And with the river, they send it out to other towns, to Novosibirsk [INAUDIBLE], the-- the-- state.
There was a big city.
Novosibirsk was a big city.
As a matter of fact, I read this week in the paper that there is a rabbi that was there, in that town, in Novosibirsk, for six, seven years.
And he was organizing Jewish life back there.
This week in the Jewish paper-- the organizer was admiring him for-- because the conditions in Russia is even worse than it was during the war.
Very bad.
So somehow he sacrifices.
He's an organizer.
I suppose he's going back now to New York or some place.
I don't remember.
Yeah.
I just read this week in-- you know, these things interest me to-- to-- to read, these things, when I see a headline like that.
What happened?
How did you find out that you were going to be let-- let go?
What-- what was-- what was going on at that time?
It came suddenly.
Of course, there was going on negotiations, probably before but we didn't know.
No paper.
If we've got a paper, we used it for cigarettes, rolling cigarettes.
[LAUGHS] That's all.
No paper.
No-- anyhow, maybe the management, the Russian management, had papers.
I don't know.
I really don't.
But we didn't know much.
It came just like a shock.
And it was good news, you know.
Good news.
We're going to be free.
We didn't know that the economical situation in Asia is worse than in Siberia.
Here you got the soup, and you got a bed-- mosquitoes, but the least a bed where to rest your head.
And we could go and take a shower once in a while.
So over there was worse even than-- than-- because there were people dying on the street for lack of food.
And somehow I managed to survive, and I don't-- work here, work there, whatever I could, went-- doing anything.
Anything you do, to survive.
Any place you come, you meet people.
You try to see, what are you doing?
What are you doing?
We go.
We went thousands of kilometers in Russian Asia just to find they said, over there is better.
So-- so we went from here, there.
No, no.
If they caught you-- because you had to buy a ticket.
Who had money to buy a ticket?
So we went on the train, and if they caught you, they throw you down.
You-- you jump on the other train, the second train coming.
And that's how we managed to-- to get along.
I worked in a gas station for a while, and but the-- the pay was so poor that you couldn't even-- the black market was flourishing in the-- in these places because there was no food.
You could buy for-- but who had money to buy?
Some people maybe had.
Did I mention to you that I met a couple of guys that were also in Russia after we came to Palestine?
And we lived in the same building.
And they said, one was the president of a bank in Russia.
Now I'm assuming he had it much better than we had, because he had everything.
You know?
They-- if you are sitting on the top, you know, you're-- first you-- As a matter of fact, we went out yesterday with some friends.
So she said, her father-- step father got a job in Russia.
He became head of the supply house.
So he gained 65 pounds from she saw him last.
And after she saw him in Germany, after the war, when they came in the camps, she saw him, and he gained 65 pounds because he had it good.
You know?
I was suffering very much, very much.
I don't know if a lot of people suffered that much, but somehow or another, I wasn't that lucky to-- to be-- to have something.
I had to struggle for every day of life.
Every-- every step of the way, I had to struggle.
But I survived.
No.
No, no.
I didn't see those friends anymore.
But we were-- you know, you make friends.
Everybody wanted somebody to talk to or to have a little somebody to talk to.
You know?
So really, not special friends, but acquaintances.
So when we met, you know, so we just did a couple of things together.
We went on the-- excuse me-- on the black market to buy something if you had.
If you-- You did everything possible, even some things that they were not legal, you do.
You do things that I don't even want to mention because you do everything for survival.
Anybody will do anything.
I can image.
The guy that they just caught in-- the guy-- the American pilot that survived in the-- so he ate mosquitoes.
And-- and-- you do anything.
Well, I remember in Asia, we were staying in line, about 300, 400 people, which was not much.
The lines were big because after a while, if they start giving a piece bread, 200, 300 people got.
The-- the strong guy got.
And the weaker guys that stayed behind never got anything.
So I remember there was a guy staying in line, and somebody wanted to get in before him, and just throw him to the floor.
And he died instantly on the sidewalk.
But that was a daily occurrence.
People died on the street.
You'd see [? laid ?] people that they couldn't take them away fast enough because the Russians itself had all the-- all the man power they had on the front lines, all the young people.
And whoever was left is just women, children, and older people.
And-- and so was-- was hard.
It was very hard.
Once we got out of Siberia.
That was the reason that they had released us.
Because otherwise they would have kept it-- kept us there.
But they released us because that was the-- the agreement that they had, that they're going to let the Polish people go and form.
And they, the Polish people, helped a lot their own people, how to-- how to go from place to place and get organized.
And that's why a lot of people went out from the same route I went.
And they came to Iran, and over there, they were free already.
They were forming a Polish army.
Of course, they didn't want to take the Jews to the army, but that's another thing, another [INAUDIBLE], I mean, in the history of--
So even the same thing, a friend of mine told me that after the war, he came to Lublin.
And it was after the war, and he survived.
And he thought, now, it's going to be better.
But it was a pogrom.
Pollocks went and made a pogrom against Jews, and they killed about 13 people at that time.
So it was a big struggle to survive as a Jew with the whole war because everybody was infected with Nazism and-- and their ideas.
You were-- so you were-- but you were traveling with-- with the Polish?
A lot of Poles, yeah.
A lot of Poles traveled with us, the ones that were arrested.
Of course, there were more, I think, more Jews than Poles because Jews were running away from Germany, even Russian Jews from all Ukraine that the Germans-- that's why they attacked.
Because Ukraine borders with Poland.
And Poland-- that's where they came from, the German army, and attacked the Russians.
To Tashkent?
Probably three weeks, also about three weeks.
And when we got there.
At least it was warm there.
You see, Siberia is cold and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] desert-like.
It's not desert, but it's no-- no inhabitants.
And over there, there was a lot of people because it was very fertile, the-- the whole area.
And they had a lot of oil there, the Russians.
And that's where they were concentrating more on this.
And that's where we went.
Because it was warmer there.
You could survive.
You could sleep outside.
You could sleep inside and not freeze to death.
So it was just [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] survivorship.
That's all there is to it.
But--
Well, in this city itself, maybe four, five days.
Then you move down because that city was so many people, and it was worse.
So you go to a little smaller town where there is less inhabitants, so it's easier to get something.
And you [? were ?] to work [? kind ?] [? of ?] odd work just to get a little something.
The Russian people, the people itself, not the government, but the people itself, were wonderful people, unbelievable, wonderful.
They felt your-- you pain.
They tried to help as much as they could.
Not much they could do because they themselves didn't have much, but whatever they could, they did.
And really, many times thanks to people that-- the Russian people, we survived.
Because they were trying to help.
And they did help a lot, just being human beings.
Because they grew up from the time of the revolution in 1914 till then, till '39, which is about-- these young people didn't know of anti-Semitism.
So they didn't ask you if you were a Jew or not Jew.
You were a Pollock, from-- from Poland, a-- a refugee.
So they did help a lot, no question about it.
They did help.
No.
No, because the language barrier and the environment and the geographic-- not so much geographic, but economical.
It wasn't the place to-- but some people did.
They met a lot of girls.
They got married there, and they stayed.
But then they went away, anyhow, to Germany, and they took their wives with.
I have a couple of friends that married in Russia.
And they were better off with their family.
It was a different life than alone.
With a family you could-- somebody cooked at home, whatever they had, even a little water to cook was all right, just to have a little warm water there.
But if you are alone, it's very hard, much harder than--
No, no.
I was not in the Polish army.
I was traveling on my own because once we went to [INAUDIBLE], and they didn't accept us in the army, so nothing you could do.
You just go on your own because they didn't feed you.
They formed kitchens, and they were feeding their people.
But didn't let us in to get some.
Oh, like I mentioned to you, we went from-- to [?
Krasnovihotk, ?] to that state.
And being in [?
Krasnovihotk, ?] the Russian authorities forced everybody on the boat-- there was a big boat-- and let everybody out.
They were calling that a frontier city, and they didn't want any enemies there, or whoever.
And thanks to that, we went out to Iran because they were forcing everybody that was in that town and was not Russian-- they were forcing on that boat.
And thanks to that, we went to Iran.
And that's how.
Over in Iran, it started a new life, entirely different.
We were in Polish camps.
We were fed well.
We-- they had, you know, all lice.
They were in the billions.
So they were disinfecting all the old clothes-- gave us a new shirt and shorts because it was hot in Iran, there, and shorts and a blanket, and we were clean already and fed.
So it was a-- a paradise.
Paradise, to compare with all we had in Russia.
Polish authorities.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were running the camps, and being Polish subjects, they had to-- they no choice because over there, they didn't want to show that they are too anti-Semitic.
[LAUGHS] So they-- and maybe influence some Jewish authorities in the world.
I don't know.
Too small to say anything about that because I really don't know.
But we didn't have it that bad.
We had it good because the British had, like I mentioned, disinfected us.
They took and burned all our clothes that we came out from Russia and gave us a pair of shorts, an undershirt, a shirt, a pair of socks, a pair of shoes, and a blanket.
That's what we got.
And that was enough.
That was enough to-- to get along.
We went-- you mean the Jews in the camp?
Oh, yeah.
We know who was Jewish and who was not.
And we were more or less-- as matter of fact, one family was there, a whole family, and they told me about that Jewish Federation in Iran, in-- in Tehran.
So we went there, and Saturday we went to the shul there, to-- to pray there.
And it was a big community of Jewish people and very wealthy people that tried to help.
They did.
They did a lot.
I wouldn't know.
There was a name, but I just couldn't recall anymore.
They were called the Jewish Federation.
As a matter of fact, they organized kitchens because at the same time-- the Israeli army, I mean, under the British rule, Palestinian-- Palestinian army-- Israel formed a brigade.
They were under the rule of the British.
And they were stationed in Iran.
So a lot of these people in-- in Iran made dinner for them.
If came a holiday, they made holidays.
And we benefited from that.
And that-- as a matter of fact, I met one of my [? landsman ?] there in the Jewish brigade, that was in the Jewish brigade army, but under the British rule.
Right.
Right.
They came, authorities from Palestine, from the Jewish agency in Palestine, and they took our names, and this, and all details.
And they gave us the issued permits to go.
And they also made a kind of arrangement with the Polish army to take us on this tour, on the trip.
So we went with the army.
We were two separate-- there were three trucks where-- from the Polish army.
But we were all these refugees that came to Palestine for that trip.
Together with them, we traveled.
We came-- went to Mosul, Iraq, and we stayed there a few days.
And from Iraq to Transjordan, and that was the route-- Iran, Iraq, Transjordan, and Palestine.
So how was-- what were the relations between the Polish army and--
Well, they had-- they weren't bad.
There was no-- of course, we didn't associate with them.
We were separated because there were, like I said, three trucks of-- truckloads of the people, the 120 people, that got certificates.
But we were traveling with them the same time, with their-- with their whole transport for Polish army.
They-- they came to Palestine.
They stationed in Tel Aviv at the time.
Then they were sent to Italy to fight, from Palestine to Italy.
It was their first battle in-- in Italy, but the Polish army participated in the fight against the Nazis.
Here and there, rumors.
You could only put two and two together.
But not exactly news about hometown or my family or nothing, no.
Well, a lot of people went to kibbutz, and a lot people went in cities.
A lot that had families, they joined their families.
But we weren't as a group anymore.
Excuse me.
We separated.
And we were two, three, four, five people that we always held together, but not-- not with families.
A lot of people had families.
One-- one of our boys had a father in-- in-- in Israel, so he went to his father.
Another one came, the day when we came, we got to Tel Aviv, and his father died the same day.
Kalisch ?], his name was.
His father died the day before or the same day.
I don't remember.
And he found out.
Well, he knew that he has a father in Israel, but after he found out that he's dead.
He died.
OK.
Can I go?
